Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted by other things—my extracurriculars, hanging out with friends, or just feeling too anxious to focus. I usually ended up cramming things in whenever I could, maybe late at night or between other commitments. I know 11 hours a week doesn't sound like much, but it felt like I was always thinking about studying even when I wasn't actually doing it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, honestly. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then life would happen and I'd end up doing most of my work right before deadlines. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly spend hours trying to catch up. The inconsistency stressed me out, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, especially when my anxiety was spiking and I felt like I was falling behind. Occasionally a classmate would mention something from the discussion board and I'd realize I needed to check it out.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, maybe when a module was particularly interesting or when I was trying to get my act together. But it never stuck as a habit. The LMS always felt like something I had to deal with rather than something that was naturally part of my day.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things or just focus on the audio. The discussion posts were okay too—sometimes hearing other students' perspectives helped things click. When the content was explained verbally rather than just written, it made way more sense to me.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit on my laptop. Written stuff takes me forever to get through and my mind wanders. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice activities because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to the exam or assignments, I'd prioritize it. Also, if something was explained in a way that made sense to me—like through audio or video—I'd actually engage with it. But if it felt like busywork or I couldn't see the point, I'd push it aside. My time felt too limited to spend on things that seemed unnecessary.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. With my anxiety levels and everything else going on, I could barely keep up with what was required. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had the pressure of consequences, which honestly was the main thing that got me to do anything.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or working out. I also had a study group outside of class where we'd talk through concepts verbally. I'd record myself summarizing topics and play it back. A lot of my actual learning happened through conversations and audio, not through clicking around the LMS.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times but the LMS would only show one view. My study group sessions were huge for my understanding but obviously left no digital trace. Sometimes I'd spend hours thinking through problems in my head or talking them out with friends. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then replay it multiple times offline. Same with any audio content—I'd save it and listen repeatedly during my commute. The LMS probably thinks I only engaged with stuff once, but I was actually revisiting material way more than it shows.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars. There were weeks where I could barely bring myself to open the LMS because I knew how behind I was and it just made me more anxious. The stress kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in, stare at the screen, and then close it without doing anything meaningful. The workload felt overwhelming sometimes, especially when assignments from multiple courses piled up. My extracurricular commitments also ate into my study time, and I'd end up sacrificing course work.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just zoning out. I'd scroll through materials without actually processing anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. The anxiety made it hard to concentrate even when I was trying.
Q15: The assignments, definitely. I'd know they were important but the anxiety of starting them made me avoid them. It's weird—I scored well when I actually did them, but getting myself to begin was the hardest part. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find another excuse. Only finished like 2% of them, which is embarrassing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due. Without that pressure, I'd drift and procrastinate. The LMS probably shows these spikes of engagement clustered around due dates. It wasn't healthy, but the deadline panic was my main motivator.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it actually made me want to engage more—like it validated that I could do this if I tried. But sometimes feedback came too late to be useful, or I was already so behind that I couldn't really apply it. The mindfulness intervention they had me do helped a bit with managing my anxiety around feedback.
Q18: The structure was okay, but having 10 online courses to juggle made it hard to give any single one enough attention. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out, but I often felt lost about priorities. The flexibility of online learning was supposed to help, but for someone like me, it just meant more opportunities to procrastinate.
Q19: Having audio and video content made things easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the sheer amount of reading-heavy materials was tough. Also, when navigation was confusing or I couldn't find what I needed quickly, I'd give up and do something else. Clear, simple organization would have helped me a lot.
Q20: That's kind of me, honestly. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then choke when it came to actually submitting assignments. The anxiety of being evaluated made me avoid the final step. It's like I could engage with learning but not with being assessed. The stakes of submission felt too high.
Q21: I think that describes me somewhat too. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity wasn't super high. A lot of my learning happened offline—listening to recordings, talking with peers, thinking things through. The LMS doesn't capture that kind of engagement at all. Someone could look minimally active but be learning plenty elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, which is useful for spotting basic engagement. But they miss everything that happens offline—the listening, the thinking, the conversations. They also can't tell if someone is genuinely focused or just mindlessly scrolling. And they definitely can't measure anxiety or motivation.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and thinking about the content even outside of study time. It's not about how many times you click on something—it's about whether it's actually sinking in and whether you want to learn more.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected my engagement, or how much learning I did through listening and conversations. It wouldn't show the internal struggle of wanting to do well but feeling paralyzed by stress. It also wouldn't capture that I actually cared about learning—I just had a really hard time showing it through traditional metrics.
